thread.title: After MEA rebuff, Canada rejects report: \u2018no evidence linking PM, Jaishankar, NSA\u2019
thread.title_full: After MEA rebuff, Canada rejects report: \u2018no evidence linking PM, Jaishankar, NSA\u2019

author: Shubhajit Roy
title: After MEA rebuff, Canada rejects report: \u2018no evidence linking PM, Jaishankar, NSA\u2019
text: In a significant move indicating an attempt to dial down tensions between Ottawa and Delhi, Canadian National Security Advisor Nathalie G Drouin, contradicting a report in The Globe and Mail daily, has said the government of Canada \u201chas not stated, nor is it aware of evidence linking\u201d Prime Minister Narendra Modi, External Affairs Minister S Jaishankar and NSA Ajit Doval to \u201cthe serious criminal activity\u201d in Canada \u2014 a reference to the killing of Khalistan separatist Hardeep Singh Nijjar last year. The Canadian NSA\u2019s statement came two days after India rejected the report in The Globe and Mail. \u201cWe do not normally comment on media reports. However, such ludicrous statements made to a newspaper, purportedly by a Canadian government source, should be dismissed with the contempt they deserve,\u201d Randhir Jaiswal, spokesperson for the Ministry of External Affairs, had said. On November 19, The Globe and Mail report stated, \u201cCanadian security agencies believe Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India knew about the killing of a Sikh separatist leader in British Columbia and other\u00a0 violent plots, according to a senior national-security official who worked on the intelligence assessment of New Delhi\u2019s foreign-interference operations in Canada.\u201d It also mentioned that Canadian and American intelligence tied the assassination operations to Union Home Minister Amit Shah. \u201cWhile Canada does not have direct evidence that Modi knew, the official said the assessment is that it would be unthinkable that three senior political figures in India would not have discussed the targeted killings with Modi before proceeding,\u201d it said. In an official statement following this report and the strong Indian response, Canadian NSA Drouin said, \u201cThe Government of Canada has not stated, nor is it aware of evidence, linking Prime Minister Modi, Minister Jaishankar, or NSA Doval to the serious criminal activity within Canada.\u201d The Canadian NSA, who is also the Deputy Clerk of the Privy Council in Canada, said, \u201cOn October 14, because of a significant and ongoing threat to public safety, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) and officials took the extraordinary step of making public accusations of serious criminal activity in Canada perpetrated by agents of the Government of India.\u201d She called The Globe and Mail report \u201cspeculative and inaccurate\u201d. Drouin\u2019s statement is significant since she has given a clean chit to her counterpart, NSA Doval, though the Trudeau administration has accused \u201cagents of the Indian government\u201d of being behind criminal activities in Canada. But Drouin has not walked back on the statement by Deputy Foreign Minister David Morrison who last month acknowledged leaking information to The Washington Post which first mentioned Shah\u2019s name, two weeks after the report was published. Morrison told a parliamentary committee that he confirmed Shah\u2019s name to The Washington Post. \u201cThe journalist called me and asked if it was that person. I confirmed it was that person,\u201d Morrison told the committee. But he did not say how Canada knew of Shah\u2019s alleged involvement. Testifying before the committee, Drouin stated that she did not need Trudeau\u2019s authorisation to leak the information. She said that the decision to leak information to The Washington Post was part of a strategy she and Morrison devised to ensure a major US outlet reported on Canada\u2019s stance in its escalating foreign-interference dispute with India. The strategy, she added, was seen by the Prime Minister\u2019s Office. Two days later, New Delhi had summoned a representative from the Canadian High Commission and lodged its strong objections regarding the serious allegations. The latest statement by Drouin is being seen as an exercise to contain the damage, and not escalating the matter to the Prime Minister\u2019s level.
